fund.name: ICICI Prudential Flexicap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This is our core growth engine. Since you’re in the 30% tax bracket, we need equity-taxed funds to lock in better long-term tax rates. This fund has beaten the benchmark for years and gives the flexibility to put money in large, mid, and small caps where we see good value.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Midcap Fund-Direct Plan-Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Midcaps offer faster growth than big companies. This fund’s manager is known for spotting high-quality mid-sized companies before they become markets. It’s tax-efficient (like all equity funds) and fits your aggressive timeline of 32 years.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: A cost-saving backup. If midcap active funds underperform, we switch to this low-cost index fund that simply buys the top 150 midcaps. Investor fees are just 0.20%, saving you cash over decades.

fund.name: Sundaram Long Term Tax Advantage Fund Series I Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: An ELSS fund for tax saving. You’re in the 30% tax bracket, so every ₹1,000 you invest here saves you ₹300 in taxes immediately. Plus, it has a 3-year lock-in and then benefits from the lower 12.5% tax on gains after 1 year. Top picks for tax savings.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Your safety net. Even with an aggressive plan, we keep 30% (15% of total) in a hybrid fund that mostly invests in stocks (plenty of equity to keep you tax-efficient) but also puts some in safe debt. Smoother ups and downs, same tax treatment as pure equity funds.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Small caps carry the highest growth potential but are very volatile. This fund has a proven track record of making money even in bad years. Your 30-year horizon is long enough to recover from any drawdown. Tax treatment is the same as other equity funds.

Buying gold is like buying digital gold. It’s a hedge if the economy struggles or inflation spikes. Unfortunately, gold funds are taxed at your slab (30%), but since it’s only 5% of your portfolio, the impact is small.

fund.name: Franklin India SHORT TERM INCOME PLAN - Direct - GROWTH

allocations.rationale: A medium-duration bond fund to stabilize things a bit. It holds short-term bonds, so it’s safer than long-term bonds but gives slightly higher returns than overnight funds. The flip side: returns grow at your tax slab (30%), which is worse than equity funds.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Multi-Asset Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This fund automatically balances between stocks, bonds, and sometimes gold. Good for your “family responsibilities” part—gives a bit of stability without a lot of manual work. Taxed at your slab.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, Coin, or MFU (any app that’s zero-commission). Step 2: Use the AMFI codes above to point to the specific funds (e.g., 148990 = ICICI Flexi). Step 3: Set up monthly SIPs for the amounts shown above every 1st of the month via auto-debit from your bank. Step 4: Do NOT top-up after the first year. Keep the amounts fixed until the ‘SIP Step-Up’ schedule kicks in (optional). Step 5: Hardest step now: forget it for a year, then do quarterly reviews as discussed below.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Verify SIPs are running correctly. Ensure auto-debit didn’t fail. Check if the correct fund options are picking ‘Direct - Growth’ only.
After 12 months: Compare each fund’s return with its category benchmark. If a fund underperforms the benchmark by more than 2% over 1 year, consider switching to a better performer in its category.

Every quarter (every 3 months): If the market has crashed >10%, do nothing. If it has pumped >20% in a quarter and your equity exposure is >40%, trim a bit by selling the unwanted equity fund and buying the debt fund to rebalance to 30% equity.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check your portfolio mix: you aimed for roughly 60% total equity (Flexic+Mid+Small+ELSS), 30% hybrid/debt, and 10% gold. If stocks have boomed and equity is now 65% instead of 60%, sell 5% of equity (pick the worst-performing one) and buy into your debt funds. If stocks have crashed and equity is 45%, do the reverse. Keep the allocation constant over time.
plan.rationale: You’re 36 with 32 years ahead. That gives you time to ride out big crashes and enjoy huge upsides. An aggressive plan with ~60% equity and ~50% going into aggressive hybrids and smallcaps fits your 32-year horizon and aggressive appetite. We added a tax-efficient mix so you don’t end up with empty pockets in 30 years. With ₹20k/SIP and ₹6.47M existing corpus, and assuming these returns, you can build far more than the ₹20C retirement target.
risks: Market risk: Stocks can drop 20-30% in a bad year. Your equity portion can go from 1C to 70L in a crash. You must not panic-sell when you see headlines. Keep the money invested.

Tax risk: You’re in the 30% slab. Debt and gold funds are taxed at 30% (no tax break). They’ll be fine, but they won’t give you the same tax shield as equity funds. That’s why we mostly use equity and ELSS.
Mismatch risk: If you stop contributing or use the money before 2028, you’ll lose the benefit of compounding. Ensure your ‘family responsibilities’ are covered before investing here.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: Only 5% of the equity allocation (Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund) is in a passive/index instrument. The rest (95%) are actively managed equity funds like ICICI Flexicap, Motilal Midcap, Nippon Small Cap, etc. Thus, the equity sleeve is overwhelmingly active.

turnover_score: The plan includes quarterly reviews with tactical triggers (e.g., trimming equity if market pumps >20% in a quarter) and annual rebalancing with active switches based on underperformance. This indicates moderate-to-high turnover, though not extreme trading. Hence, low passivity score.

cost_emphasis_score: Cost is mentioned only in the context of the index fund as a 'cost-saving backup' and with a note on its 0.20% fees. However, cost is not the primary driver for most fund selections — performance and manager skill are emphasized more. Still, some cost awareness exists, warranting a mid-range score.

research_vs_cost_score: Fund selection is based on manager track record ('manager is known for spotting high-quality mid-sized companies'), fund performance vs benchmark, and active conviction ('core growth engine', 'proven track record'). This reflects deep research orientation, not broad market exposure. Thus, very low passivity.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The plan is designed for a 32-year horizon, with long-term compounding in mind. SIPs are set-and-forget, and investors are advised to 'forget it for a year'. Rebalancing is infrequent and structural. Strong long-term orientation.

portfolio_activeness_score: All equity funds are actively managed with rationales centered on manager skill and outperformance. The only passive fund is a small 5% hedge. Hybrid and debt allocations are actively managed (e.g., Franklin Short Term Income, ICICI Equity & Debt). No risk metrics are provided, but category and rationale confirm active management across the board. Hence, very low passivity.

pqs.reasoning:
The investment plan is highly aligned with the investor's aggressive risk appetite, long 32-year horizon, and retirement goal of building a ₹20Cr corpus. The equity-heavy allocation (starting at 60% and increasing to 80%) is justified by the long time frame and growth objective, and the inclusion of ELSS for tax savings is appropriate given the investor is in the 30% tax slab under the old regime. The plan shows strong internal consistency: rationale, allocations, rebalancing, and risk disclosures align coherently.

Diversification is good across market caps (large, mid, small), fund types (flexi-cap, midcap, small-cap, hybrid, multi-asset), and asset classes (equity, debt, gold). However, there is some concentration in Motilal Oswal funds (two midcap funds) and ICICI (three funds), and the 10% allocation to a single small-cap fund adds volatility risk, slightly reducing diversification score.

The risk-return profile is appropriate for an aggressive investor. Projected returns (11.1% base) are realistic for an equity-heavy portfolio. Downside risks are acknowledged, and rebalancing rules are provided.

Tax efficiency is strong but not optimal. The plan correctly emphasizes equity-taxed funds (≥65% equity) to benefit from LTCG at 12.5% and STCG at 20%, avoiding slab taxation. ELSS is wisely included for 80C benefits. However, the 10% allocation to Franklin India Short Term Income Plan (debt fund) is suboptimal: as a 30% slab investor, this fund will be taxed at marginal rate, eroding returns. A better choice would have been an arbitrage or aggressive hybrid fund (which are equity-taxed) for the same risk profile. The 5% gold ETF is correctly flagged as slab-taxed, but no attempt is made to minimize this drag (e.g., via physical gold or SGBs with indexation). The 5% multi-asset fund is also likely slab-taxed if it doesn't meet equity criteria, further reducing tax efficiency. Thus, while tax awareness is present, optimization is incomplete.
